text:
Examination of a Social
Problem-Solving Intervention
to Treat Selective Mutism

Mark O’Reilly
The University of Texas at Austin
Deirdre McNally
University College Dublin
Jeff Sigafoos
The University of Tasmania
Giulio E. Lancioni
University of Bari
Vanessa Green
The University of Tasmania
Chaturi Edrisinha
St. Cloud State University
Wendy Machalicek
Audrey Sorrells
Russell Lang
The University of Texas at Austin
Robert Didden
Radbound University Nijmegen

The authors examined the use of a social problem-solving intervention to
treat selective mutism with 2 sisters in an elementary school setting. Both
girls were taught to answer teacher questions in front of their classroom peers
during regular classroom instruction. Each girl received individualized
instruction from a therapist and was taught to discriminate salient social cues,
select an appropriate social response, perform the response, and evaluate her
performance. The girls generalized the skills to their respective regular
classrooms and maintained the skills for up to 3 months after the removal of
the intervention. Experimental control was demonstrated using a multiple

Behavior Modification

O’Reilly et al. / A Social Skills Problem-Solving Intervention
183

baseline design across participants. Limitations of this study and issues for
future research are discussed.

Keywords:
selective mutism; behavior therapy; social problem-solving
strategies; assessment/treatment of challenging behavior; social
competence

S
elective mutism is diagnosed when a child persistently fails to speak in
specific social situations where there is an expectation to speak (e.g., in
classroom settings). Additionally, the child consistently speaks in other social
situations (e.g., at home with mom). Selective mutism is included under the
category of disorders first diagnosed in infancy, childhood, and adolescence of
the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (4th ed., DSM–IV;
American Psychiatric Association, 1994). It is a rare condition, occurring in
less than 1% of the population (Krysanski, 2003). Some research indicates that
the prevalence may be higher but that the condition goes undiagnosed
(Kumpulainen, Rasanen, Raaska, & Samppi, 1998). Selective mutism often
becomes evident when children attend school, but there tends to be a delay in
referring these children for specialist treatment, with the average age of refer-
ral being 9 years of age in one study (Kumpulainen et al., 1998). 
This condition can have negative long-term social effects for the child
if untreated (Kolvin & Goodyer, 1982). Negative educational outcomes
have also been implicated in several reviews of this disorder, but no clear
empirical evidence has been provided to support this proposal (Stone,
Kratochwill, Sladezcek, & Serlin, 2002). Several intervention protocols
have been reported in the literature and include behavior therapy, family sys-
tems therapy, psychodynamic therapy, and medication (Stone et al., 2002).
Unfortunately, the majority of studies published to date have not used rigor-
ous experimental methodologies to clearly isolate the effects of treatment
variables. Stone et al. (2002) concluded that behavior therapy has the pre-
dominance of empirical support over other treatments for this condition.
Behavioral interventions have included such strategies as reinforcement for
speaking, extinction of nonverbal attempts at communication, shaping, and
modeling appropriate behavior (e.g., Amari, Slifer, Gerson, Schenck, &
Kane, 1999; Kehle, Owen, & Cressy, 1990). In spite of the potential advan-
tages, the behavior therapy literature itself is difficult to interpret, as much
of the published reports involve case studies that do not use rigorous single-
case experimental design methodologies. Stone et al. (2002) concluded that
future treatment research with this population should incorporate more rig-
orous experimental designs to clearly evaluate treatment effects.

One form of behavioral intervention that has not been reported in the
treatment of selective mutism is the use of social problem-solving strate-
gies. Social problem-solving interventions involve teaching the individual a
generic set of verbal rules that are intended to prompt him or her to (a) decode
or discriminate salient social stimuli; (b) identify alternative social behav-
iors and determine the most appropriate among them for the social situation
at hand; (c) perform the social behavior; and (d) evaluate the effectiveness
of the social behavior once it has been performed (cf. O’Reilly, Cannella,
Sigafoos, & Lancioni, 2006). Several authors have argued that problem
solving may be an effective method for promoting the generalization and
maintenance of social skills because the participant is taught a generic set
of social rules that can be adapted to different social interactions (Gumpel,
1994; O’Reilly et al., 2006). Individuals may therefore be able to use the
verbal rules to manage their social behavior after training.
Social problem-solving interventions have successfully been used to
teach social skills to a variety of populations, including individuals with
intellectual disabilities and traumatic brain injuries (e.g., Foxx, Martella, &
Marchand-Martella, 1989; O’Reilly, Lancioni, Gardiner, Tiernan, & Lacey,
2002; O’Reilly, Lancioni & O’Kane, 2000). These interventions have pro-
duced very robust outcomes, with social skills generalizing across regular
school settings and vocational settings (e.g., Foxx, Faw, & Weber, 1991;
O’Reilly & Chasdey-Rusch, 1992; O’Reilly et al., 2002). Additionally, sev-
eral studies have demonstrated that such interventions can produce long-
term positive changes in social behavior (Foxx & Faw, 1992; O’Reilly,
Lancioni, & Kierans, 2000). For example, in a previous study, we examined
a social problem-solving intervention with two school children with intel-
lectual disabilities who were described by their teachers as being withdrawn
during class (O’Reilly & Glynn, 1995). Both students were successfully
taught to initiate and respond with their teachers during regular classroom
routines. They also began to generalize social initiations to other contexts
within the school (e.g., with peers during recess) and maintained these
skills after the removal of intervention. 
In the present study, we attempted to evaluate the effectiveness of this
social problem-solving intervention protocol with two elementary school
children (sisters) who had been formally diagnosed with selective mutism.
We attempted to use the intervention to teach the students to respond to
teacher questions during routine classroom activities. We evaluated the
effectiveness of this intervention using a multiple baseline experimental
design across participants (Kazdin, 1982). Maintenance of treatment effects
after the removal on the intervention was also evaluated.

184
Behavior Modification
Method
Participants, Settings, Therapist, and Sessions

Saoirse and Siobhan were sisters, aged 5 and 7 years, respectively. They
both attended their local elementary school in a designated socially disad-
vantaged area of a city in Ireland. Approximately 500 students attended
the school, with 15 students in each classroom. Saoirse and Siobhan were
enrolled in kindergarten and first grade, respectively. Audible speech was
measured in the classroom during regular subjects (e.g., English, Irish,
mathematics, playtime). Training in social problem-solving skills occurred
individually for the sisters in a resource room removed from the classroom.
This room contained a table with four chairs, a sofa, and various children’s
games and toys. 
Both sisters were formally diagnosed with selective mutism by a con-
sultant psychiatrist using the diagnostic criteria of the DSM–IV. No assess-
ment of intelligence was available, but both girls were believed to be
functioning in the normal range according to their teachers. Neither girl had
ever spoken in class, according to teacher reports. The mother reported that
the children were vocal at home but were reluctant to speak when in public
settings (e.g., grocery store). 
Deirdre McNally acted as primary therapist and classroom observer. She
was a certified teacher who was undergoing postgraduate training in edu-
cational psychology. Saoirse and Siobhan’s classroom teachers conducted
interobserver reliability checks throughout the study. Sessions normally
took place 2 days/week in the mornings. Social problem-solving training
sessions and classroom observations lasted approximately 30 minutes each.
Training of Therapist and Teachers

The therapist was trained by Mark O’Reilly to accurately score the tar-
get behavior and implement the training protocol before the study. A series
of three 5-minute video vignettes of elementary school classrooms were
used to score the target behavior (see target behavior description below). A
total of nine student responses (three responses in each video vignette) were
scored independently by the therapist and first author (D. M. and M. O.,
respectively). There were approximately an equal number of audible and
inaudible student responses across the vignettes. The therapist and first
author observed the video vignettes simultaneously but independently and
scored each audible student response. After the observations, the results

O’Reilly et al. / A Social Skills Problem-Solving Intervention
185

were compared and discrepancies were discussed. Training continued until
there was 100% agreement on all student responses in each of the video
vignettes. 
Mark O’Reilly initially provided the therapist (Deirdre McNally) with a
written task analysis of the training procedures. O’Reilly and McNally then
role-played the social problem-solving training protocol. The training pro-
cedures were modeled by O’Reilly while McNally observed. The therapist
then performed the training procedures while O’Reilly acted as a student.
Correct therapist responses were followed by verbal praise, and incorrect
responses were corrected. This process continued until the therapist per-
formed the training protocol correctly on three consecutive trials. 
Teachers were given a written description of the target behavior before
the study. The therapist then clarified any confusion that the teachers had
regarding the target behavior.
Target Behavior

Audible vocalizations constituted the target behavior. These were defined
as any speech that was recognized by the observer as a word, phrase, or sen-
tence at normal conversational volume in response to a request from the
teacher to the student during classroom activities. Correctness of the student
response was not evaluated.
Establishing Verbalizations With the Therapist

Before baseline and intervention, it was deemed necessary to establish
audible speech with the therapist. Each of the girls met individually with
the therapist in a resource room for eight 30-minute sessions over a 5-week
period. Highly preferred items such as puzzles, candy, painting materials,
and other toys were present in the room. The children’s mother selected
these preferred items. For the first two sessions, the girls could gain access
to any of these materials by indicating nonverbally to the therapist. At the
beginning of the third session, the therapist provided a rationale for speak-
ing in school (e.g., to let people know what you want, to make friends, to
answer the teacher properly). The therapist then indicated that they needed
to make a verbal request to access the desired items. The girls verbally
requested the items during the remainder of the sessions and played com-
fortably with the therapist.

186
Behavior Modification
Experimental Design

A multiple baseline design across participants was used to demonstrate
experimental control (Kazdin, 1982). Participants were randomly assigned
to each leg of the design. A baseline condition was implemented until sta-
ble performance in the target behavior was observed for both participants.
Social problem-solving training was then implemented for Siobhan, and the
baseline condition was continued for Saoirse. Once a visible change in the
data was evident for Siobhan and baseline data remained stable for Saoirse,
the intervention was introduced for Saoirse.
Experimental Procedures

At the beginning of each school day, the therapist met with each girl’s
teachers. Each teacher identified classroom lessons and five questions that
they would ask the student during those lessons for the 30-minute classroom
observation later that day. These questions would require Siobhan and Saoirse
to perform the target behavior in the presence of the entire class. The therapist
then used these lessons and teacher questions to train the social problem-
solving procedures to each girl. These training sessions lasted approximately
30 minutes. The lesson plans and the teacher questions used during social
problem-solving training varied from day to day. After each social problem-
solving training session, the therapist and student returned to the regular class-
room. The classroom teacher then implemented the previously agreed-upon
lessons and asked the same five questions that were targeted during the pre-
vious problem-solving training session.

Baseline. Immediately before the baseline condition, the therapist sat at
the back of each girl’s class for ten 30-minute periods over 5 weeks. This
was done to reduce the reactivity of the students in the classrooms to an out-
side observer. After this 5-week period, formal baseline observations were
conducted. During each baseline probe, the teacher asked five questions
related to the curriculum that the student was required to answer in the pres-
ence of the entire class. The therapist sat at the back of the classroom for
30 minutes and recorded the target behavior.

Social problem-solving training. Each training session began with a
rationale for learning to speak audibly in class (to answer your teacher
appropriately, to make friends etc.). The therapist then described the class-
room lesson that would occur in the student’s classroom immediately after
training (e.g., math). The therapist then stated that she and the student

O’Reilly et al. / A Social Skills Problem-Solving Intervention
187

would practice five questions about the lesson and that the teacher would
ask these questions later in class. The therapist then stated the first question
(e.g., “If I had 12 bananas and a monkey ate 7, how many bananas would I
have left?”). The therapist modeled the social rules and appropriate
response (see Appendix).
The therapist and student then role-played the skills, verbalizing the
social rules and appropriate response. The therapist delivered verbal praise
for each rule and response performed correctly by the student. Student
errors or omissions were interrupted and modeled correctly by the therapist.
This procedure was repeated for each of the five questions. Immediately
after training, the student and therapist returned to the classroom. The pro-
tocol in the classroom was identical to baseline conditions.

Follow-up probes. The teachers conducted follow-up probes twice per
week for 3 months after the removal of the problem-solving intervention.
These probes were identical to the classroom probes during baseline. The
therapist was not present in the classroom to record the target behavior dur-
ing follow-up. The teachers reported the results of these probes to the ther-
apist by phone on a weekly basis. No social problem-solving sessions were
conducted before these classroom follow-up probes.

Reliability checks. Interobserver reliability checks on the target behavior
were conducted during all sessions of the baseline and social problem-solving
conditions. The therapist and teachers independently recorded the presence
or absence of the target behavior in response to each teacher question;
[number of agreements/(the number of agreements + disagreements)] × 100%.
Interobserver reliability was 100%.
Results

The results of the experiment are presented in Figure 1. The girls were
given five opportunities to answer the teacher audibly during each class-
room observation session. The data are graphed as the percentage of oppor-
tunities performed correctly by the Siobhan and Saoirse during each
classroom observation session. Neither of the girls responded to teacher
questions during the baseline condition. With the introduction of the inter-
vention for Siobhan, there was an immediate increase in her audible
responses to the teacher questions. In fact, Siobhan answered all five ques-
tions in the classroom after the first problem-solving session. Her class-
room performance was somewhat variable for the next seven sessions.

188
Behavior Modification

O’Reilly et al. / A Social Skills Problem-Solving Intervention
189

However, from Session 9 onward, she answered all teacher questions
during classroom observations.
Saoirse showed similar patterns of responding, with no responses during
baseline followed by an immediate increase in responding during interven-
tion. A total of 17 classroom observation sessions were conducted with

Figure 1
Percentage of Opportunities Performed Correctly for Siobhan and
Saoirse During Classroom Observation Sessions
Classroom Observation Sessions
Saoirse
Siobhan
Percentage Of Opportunities Performed Correctly

Baseline
Intervention

Saoirse during the intervention phase. She answered all teacher questions
correctly during 12 of these observation sessions. She failed to answer any
teacher questions in only 2 of the intervention observation sessions.
Follow-up observations by the teachers were equally positive. Teachers
reported that Siobhan and Saoirse responded to all questions during class-
room sessions. Informal comments from the teachers indicated that the girls
were much more responsive and involved in classroom activities. The
teachers also indicated that the intervention was very effective, as both of
these students had never previously talked in school. Teachers did note,
however, that the girls remained withdrawn during unstructured school
activities such as recess.
Discussion

The results of this study demonstrate the potential effectiveness of a social
problem-solving intervention to successfully treat selective mutism. Both
girls had not spoken in class for at least 1 year before treatment. There was
an immediate and dramatic change in audible vocalizations once the inter-
vention was implemented, with both children successfully responding to
teacher questions in the classroom context. The children continued with high
levels of correct responding during the intervention period. Additionally, the
girls maintained these skills for up to 3 months after the withdrawal of the
intervention according to teacher reports. Our results, although preliminary,
are encouraging, as selective mutism is difficult to treat (Kolvin & Fundudis,
1981; Standart & La Couteur, 2003; Stone et al., 2002).
These findings add to a growing body of evidence on the effectiveness of
social problem-solving interventions to promote acquisition, generalization,
and maintenance of appropriate social skills in real-world settings for people
with a variety of social deficits (e.g., Foxx & Faw, 1992; O’Reilly, Lancioni,
& Kierans, 2000). All previous studies have examined this intervention
strategy with either rehabilitation clients or individuals with intellectual
disabilities. This is the first known study to utilize a social problem-solving
intervention to treat selective mutism.
The teachers noted a number of strengths of this problem-solving inter-
vention. First, it was relatively simple to implement and produced quick
results. Both girls began verbalizing appropriately in class after the first
30-minute training session. Second, the intervention was relatively unob-
trusive. The children were removed from the classroom, and the interven-
tion was conducted individually during brief sessions in a resource room.

190
Behavior Modification

Some behavioral interventions may include such strategies as the extinction
(ignoring) of nonverbal responses and reinforcement of appropriate responses
in the classroom. Such public interventions in the presence of the entire
class may actually exacerbate these social difficulties, as selective mutism
is often associated with social phobia (Black & Uhde, 1992). In fact, we
believe that a key component of our intervention was the establishment of
a rapport between the therapist and the girls. The play sessions immediately
before the intervention were designed not only to elicit vocalizations from
the girls but also to establish a comfortable and trusting relationship between
the therapist and the girls. 
In this study, we used a multiple baseline across-participants design to
demonstrate a functional relationship between the treatment and change in
the target behavior. The intervention was systematically staggered across
the two participants. Changes in vocalizations within the classroom occurred
only at that point when the intervention was introduced. This relationship
between changes in classroom behavior with the introduction of the inter-
vention was replicated across both girls. Such single-case designs are rela-
tively simple yet powerful tools for examining treatment effects (see
Kazdin, 1982). These designs are ideal for conducting intervention research
with low-incidence populations such as individuals with selective mutism. 
Although the findings of this research are promising, future research
should continue to investigate the effectiveness of social problem-solving
strategies in treating selective mutism. For example, it would be instructive
to examine whether teachers themselves could adapt this intervention strat-
egy for use in regular classroom contexts. Teachers may be able to meet
briefly with children before the beginning of class to role-play questions
and answers for the upcoming class period. Such adaptations in treatment
protocols may eliminate or at least reduce the level of input from specialist
services such as those of school psychologists and resource teachers.
Teachers in this study did note that, although the findings were quite posi-
tive in the classroom context, there seemed to be little generalization of
skills to interactions with peers during unstructured school periods (e.g., recess).
Future research may examine strategies to target vocalization across multiple
criterion settings. For example, children could practice vocalizations using
scenarios from the classroom and from recess. Peer confederates may
then be involved to prompt the targeted vocalizations in the recess setting
after training.
In summary, we examined the use of a social skills problem-solving
strategy to teach appropriate vocalizations within classroom settings to two
girls with selective mutism. The intervention produced immediate positive

O’Reilly et al. / A Social Skills Problem-Solving Intervention
191

192
Behavior Modification

changes in vocalizations with both girls in their respective classrooms. The
girls maintained the skills for 3 months after the removal of the interven-
tion. The efficacy of the intervention was systematically evaluated with a
multiple baseline across-participants design. These initial findings appear
to hold promise for the use of social problem-solving interventions for
students with selective mutism.

Appendix
Examples of Questions and Statements Trained for
Decoding, Deciding, Performing, and Evaluating During
Problem-Solving Training
Decoding

What’s happening?
The teacher has asked me a question.
Deciding

What should I do?
I could ignore and not answer the teacher.
I could answer the teacher because I know the right answer.
I will answer the teacher.
Performing
“Five bananas, Miss.”
Evaluating

What happened when I answered the teacher?
She was happy.
Did I do the right thing?
Yes.

Behavior Modification

Mark O’Reilly is a professor of special education at The University of Texas at Austin. He
received his PhD in Special Education from the University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign
in 1992. His research and teaching interests include curriculum and pedagogy for students
with autism and developmental disability, assessment and treatment of challenging behavior,
and development and evaluation of assistive technology.

Deirdre McNally is now an educational psychologist with the Irish Department of Education
and Science. She is interested in assessment and intervention strategies to support children
with diverse needs in regular classroom settings.

Jeff Sigafoos received his PhD in educational psychology from the University of Minnesota in
1990. His most recent appointment is as professor of education at the University of Tasmania,
Tasmania, Australia. He teaches in the area of developmental disabilities and conducts research
on communication intervention, use of assistive technology, and assessment and treatment of
challenging behaviors in persons with developmental and physical disabilities.

Giulio E. Lancioni, PhD, is a professor at the Department of Psychology, University of Bari,
Bari, Italy. His research interests include the development and evaluation of assistive tech-
nologies, social skills training, and strategies for examining and teaching choice and prefer-
ence with individuals with severe/profound and multiple disabilities.

Vanessa Green received her PhD in 1999 from the Queensland University of Technology,
Brisbane, Australia. She is currently a lecturer in education at the University of Tasmania. She
teaches in the areas of educational psychology and early childhood education. Her research
focuses on developmental and intervention issues in children’s social competence.

Chaturi Edrisinha received her PhD in special education (autism & developmental disabili-
ties) from The University of Texas at Austin in 2006. She is currently on faculty as an assis-
tant professor in the Department of Community Psychology, St. Cloud State University. She
teaches classes in principles of behavior analysis and applied behavior analysis. Her area of
research is focused on enhancing adaptive and leisure skills for persons with developmental
disabilities and addressing challenging behavior.

Wendy Machalicek is currently pursuing a PhD in special education at The University of
Texas at Austin. Her research interests include the assessment and treatment of challenging
behavior in persons with intellectual disabilities, the development of social validity measures
for treatments, and the use of videoconferencing technology to improve staff development.

Audrey Sorrells, PhD, is an associate professor in special education and fellow in the Lee
Hage Jamail Regents Chair in Education at The University of Texas at Austin. She has 25 years
of experience as a teacher educator and classroom teacher of diverse students with disabilities.
She received her PhD in special education and educational leadership at the University of
Florida. She is co-editor of Multiple Voices for Ethnically Diverse Exceptional Learners and
has published numerous articles, books, and book chapters. Her research includes validating
instructional strategies for middle school struggling readers in diverse classrooms, as well as
professional development for teachers of diverse students in inclusive settings.

Russell Lang is currently a doctoral student at The University of Texas at Austin. He has expe-
rience working in the schools as a behavior specialist and is a board-certified behavior analyst.
His research interests include the assessment and treatment of challenging behaviors and the
use of assistive technology with persons with developmental disabilities.

Robert Didden, PhD, is an associate professor at the Department of Special Education at the
Radboud University Nijmegen, Nijmegen, the Netherlands. He is also working as a psycholo-
gist at Trajectum-Hanzeborg, a center for people with mild intellectual disabilities and severe
behavior disorders at Zutphen, the Netherlands. His research interests include challenging
behaviors such as developmental disabilities, applied behavior analysis, and sleep disorders.

O’Reilly et al. / A Social Skills Problem-Solving Intervention
195
